title: Tax and Shares in NZ
objectives: Explain NZ's tax treatment of dividends including imputation credits
Describe the Portfolio Investment Entity (PIE) tax regime for funds
Explain why NZ has no capital gains tax on share investments (in most circumstances)

vocabulary.definition: NZ does not generally tax profits made from selling shares, property or other investments — unless the investment was bought with the intention to resell (the 'bright-line' test applies to property but generally not shares).
A tax credit attached to dividends reflecting the company tax already paid. Passes through to shareholders to prevent double taxation on the same income.
A NZ tax structure for managed funds (like KiwiSaver and index funds) that caps the tax rate on investment returns at the investor's prescribed investor rate (PIR), maximum 28%.
Your personal tax rate for PIE investments — either 10.5%, 17.5% or 28% depending on your income.
The NZ tax regime for investments in offshore companies (like US shares). Taxes based on 5% of the value of the holding each year regardless of whether dividends are paid.

activities.title: The Good News — NZ Has No Capital Gains Tax on Shares
activities.notePrompt: Write: What does 'no capital gains tax' mean for NZ share investors? What is the one exception and does it apply to ordinary investors holding diversified portfolios?
activities.content:
One of the most investor-friendly aspects of NZ's tax system is that there is generally no capital gains tax (CGT) on shares.

In plain English: if you buy 1,000 Mainfreight shares at $50 and sell them 10 years later for $120, your $70,000 capital gain is completely tax-free.

This is remarkable compared to many countries: the US taxes long-term capital gains at 0% to 23.8% depending on income. Australia taxes them at marginal income rates (with a 50% discount for assets held over 12 months). The UK taxes them at 10% to 28%.

The NZ exception — the 'share trader' rule: If you are classified as a professional trader who buys shares with the primary purpose of reselling them at a profit, your gains may be taxable as income. This applies to high-frequency traders and investment businesses — not to ordinary investors holding a diversified portfolio for growth.

IRD factors for classifying a share trader include: frequency of transactions, purpose of purchase, length of holding, and whether trading is your occupation.

For the vast majority of NZ investors — people holding a diversified portfolio of shares for medium to long-term growth — there is no CGT. This makes starting early and holding long-term even more attractive in NZ than in most countries.

activities.title: Dividends and Imputation Credits
activities.notePrompt: If you are on a 33% income tax rate and receive a $70 dividend with a $30 imputation credit (30/70 = 28% company tax), what additional tax do you pay?
activities.content:
NZ dividends come with imputation credits attached — a system designed to prevent double taxation.

How it works:
1. Spark earns $100 profit.
2. Spark pays 28% company tax = $28 to IRD. Profit after tax = $72.
3. Spark distributes $72 as a dividend with $28 of imputation credits attached.
4. You (the shareholder) receive $72 cash + $28 tax credit = $100 of 'gross dividend'.
5. Your income tax on $100 at 30% personal rate = $30.
6. Minus $28 imputation credit = only $2 additional tax to pay.

Full vs partial imputation: If a company paid the full 28% company tax, the dividend is 'fully imputed.' Some companies (particularly overseas subsidiaries) may have partial imputation or none.

For low-income investors (17.5% tax rate): the imputation credit ($28) exceeds their tax ($17.50) — they receive a $10.50 tax refund on the dividend. This makes NZ dividends very tax-efficient for lower-income shareholders.

For KiwiSaver and PIE funds: dividends flow into the fund and are taxed at the investor's PIR (max 28%), which matches the company tax rate — so imputation credits often fully offset any tax on the fund's NZ dividend income.

activities.statement: All NZ investors pay the same 28% tax rate on returns from PIE funds like KiwiSaver.
activities.explanation: False. PIE funds (including KiwiSaver funds) tax investors at their Prescribed Investor Rate (PIR), which depends on individual income. The three PIR rates are 10.5% (for those earning under $14,000 annually), 17.5% (earning $14,001 to $48,000), and 28% (earning above $48,000 or those with investment income). Students and young investors in their first years of work often qualify for the 10.5% or 17.5% PIR — making PIE funds particularly tax-efficient for them.

activities.title: Overseas Shares and the FIF Rules
activities.notePrompt: Write: Why does NZ have a special tax rule for overseas shares? If you hold $40,000 of US shares, how much income is you assumed to have earned under FIF rules?
activities.content:
Buying US, Australian or other overseas shares through platforms like Hatch or Sharesies introduces a different NZ tax regime: the Foreign Investment Fund (FIF) rules.

Why FIF exists: Without special rules, NZ investors in overseas shares could defer NZ tax indefinitely by buying non-dividend-paying growth stocks (like Amazon or Alphabet). FIF prevents this by taxing a deemed return on overseas shareholdings annually.

How FIF works:
— If your overseas share portfolio is over NZD $50,000, FIF rules apply.
— Under the default FIF method, 5% of the opening market value of your overseas holdings is treated as taxable income each year — regardless of whether dividends were actually paid.
— This 5% deemed income is added to your ordinary income and taxed at your marginal rate.

Example: You hold $60,000 of US shares on 1 April (tax year start). FIF deems: $60,000 × 5% = $3,000 of income. At 30% tax rate: $900 tax regardless of actual dividends or gains.

Exceptions:
— Australian shares listed on the ASX with DTA (Double Tax Agreement): exempt from FIF, treated like NZ shares
— Portfolio below NZD $50,000 in overseas shares: FIF does not apply

For most young NZ investors starting out with small amounts, the $50,000 threshold means FIF rules will not apply for many years.

questions.question: Jordan buys 500 Spark shares at $4.10, holds for 4 years, and sells at $5.40. His capital gain is $650. How much NZ income tax does he pay on this gain?
options: $195 — 30% capital gains tax on the $650 profit
$0 — NZ does not have a capital gains tax on ordinary share investments held for growth
$117 — 18% reduced rate for long-term capital gains
$650 — all capital gains are treated as ordinary income and taxed at the full marginal rate
questions.explanation: NZ has no capital gains tax on ordinary share investments. Jordan's $650 gain is completely tax-free. This is one of NZ's most investor-friendly tax features. The exception (share trader rules) does not apply to Jordan — he held shares for 4 years as a long-term investor, not a professional trader seeking to resell for profit.

questions.question: You receive a fully imputed dividend of $72 from Spark NZ. The attached imputation credit is $28. If your income tax rate is 17.5%, how much additional tax do you pay (or refund do you receive)?
options: Pay $17.50 additional tax — 17.5% on the $100 gross dividend
Pay $12.60 additional tax — 17.5% on the $72 cash dividend received
Receive a $10.50 refund — your 17.5% tax on $100 ($17.50) is less than the $28 imputation credit
Pay $28 tax — dividends are always taxed at the company tax rate of 28%
questions.explanation: Gross dividend = $72 cash + $28 imputation credit = $100. Your tax at 17.5%: $100 × 17.5% = $17.50. Imputation credit available: $28. $17.50 tax due − $28 credit = −$10.50 (refund). Because the company already paid 28% tax and your personal rate is only 17.5%, the excess credit is refunded to you. NZ imputation credits are very efficient for lower-income investors.

activities.title: Which Tax Rule Applies? Match the Scenario

pairs.item: You sell Mainfreight shares after 5 years for a $4,000 profit
pairs.zone: No tax — no capital gains tax in NZ

pairs.item: You receive a Spark dividend with imputation credits
pairs.zone: Taxed as income, imputation credits offset tax

pairs.item: You hold $80,000 of Apple shares (US) — FIF rules apply
pairs.zone: 5% of opening value deemed as taxable income

pairs.item: KiwiSaver growth fund returns taxed at your PIR
You hold $30,000 of US shares — below the FIF threshold
pairs.zone: No FIF — taxed on actual dividends received

activities.explanation: NZ's tax treatment of investments varies significantly by investment type. Capital gains on NZ shares are generally tax-free. Dividends are taxed as income but imputation credits prevent double taxation. Overseas shares above $50,000 are taxed under FIF rules. KiwiSaver and PIE funds cap tax at 28%. Understanding these rules helps optimise investment decisions.

activities.statement: The NZ Foreign Investment Fund (FIF) rules tax you on capital gains actually made from overseas shares.
activities.explanation: False. FIF rules do not tax actual capital gains. Instead, they deem 5% of the opening value of your overseas portfolio as income each year — regardless of what actually happened (gains, losses or dividends). You could have lost 20% on your US shares but still owe FIF tax on the deemed 5% income. Conversely, you could have gained 40% but only pay tax on 5%. FIF is a simplified regime that trades accuracy for administrative simplicity.

activities.question: What is the NZ tax regime for managed funds including KiwiSaver that caps investor tax rates based on their income level?
activities.placeholder: Type the three letters or full name...
activities.hint: Three-letter abbreviation — think of a circular pastry.
activities.explanation: PIE (Portfolio Investment Entity) is the NZ tax structure for managed funds including KiwiSaver. Investors are taxed at their PIR (Prescribed Investor Rate) — 10.5%, 17.5% or 28% — rather than their full marginal income tax rate. This is particularly beneficial for investors who pay marginal rates above 28% on other income, and for those who qualify for the 10.5% PIR on low incomes.

activities.title: NZ's Tax Advantages for Investors
activities.prompt: Compared to countries like Australia (which does have capital gains tax) and the US, NZ has several tax advantages for share investors. List three specific NZ tax advantages for a young investor. How might these advantages change the decision to invest in NZ shares versus overseas shares? Are there any disadvantages to NZ's tax approach?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — understanding the tax environment shapes which investments are most efficient for NZ investors.
